Mainland Australia is the main landmass of the Australian continent, excluding surrounding islands and territories.
The area of mainland Australia is 7,591,608 km2 (2,931,136 sq mi), covering about 98% of Australia's total area and its population.
Australia separated from the supercontinent Gondwana around 130 million years ago, with full separation from Antarctica occurring around 53 million years ago.
Australian Aborigines occupied mainland Australia up to 50,000 to 60,000 years ago, making them one of the oldest living populations in the world outside of Africa.
Over 500 different clan groups with unique cultures, beliefs, and languages inhabited mainland Australia prior to European arrival in the late 18th century.
The first documented encounter of Australia by Willem Janszoon occurred in 1606, followed by further European explorations leading to the establishment of a British penal colony at Botany Bay.
Australia is located on the Indo-Australian tectonic plate and is not influenced by severe tectonic activity.
The four primary landform divisions of Australia are the Coastal Plains, Eastern and Southern Highlands, Central Lowlands (and Deserts), and Western and Northern Plateaus (and Basins).
The Coastal Plains along the Eastern seaboard range from Queensland to Victoria and are home to major cities like Brisbane, Sydney, and Melbourne.
Southern Highlands are located across New South Wales, the Australian Capital Territory, and Victoria.
Southern Highlands experience snowfall for more than half the year during winter.
Central Lowlands occupy approximately 25% of the Australian mainland and have an average height of under 200 metres above sea level.
Central Lowlands includes three primary drainage basins: Carpentaria Lowlands in the North, Lake Eyre at the center, and Murray-Darling Basin in the South.
Lake Eyre is the lowest point on the mainland at 50 meters below sea level.
The Western Plateau was formed approximately 144 to 65 million years ago during the Cretaceous period from the evaporation of a great inland sea.
One-third of the mainland consists of Plateaus made of igneous and metamorphic rock up to a billion years old.
The Plateau is an area of tablelands averaging 500 meters above sea level.
Notable geographical features of the Plateau include Uluru, Kata Tjuta, the Finke River, the Victorian Desert, Great Sandy Desert, and Gibson Desert.
Western Plateau receives minimal rainfall and appears red in color.
Northern Plateau experiences heavy rainfall throughout the wet season from November to April.
Northern Plateau is located in a more tropical region compared to the Western Plateau.
Australia's geological processes led to the accumulation of mineral, oil, and gas resources over millions of years.
Australia is one of the world's largest producers of iron ore, coal, bauxite, manganese, nickel, copper, gold, silver, cobalt, lead, uranium, opal, and diamond.
As of 2021, the majority of Australia's electricity generation came from renewable energy sources, particularly wind and solar.
Liberal National opposition has never controlled all of Mainland Australia at federal and state levels simultaneously as of 2024.

Five colonies were established on the Australian mainland from 1788 to 1859, along with one in Tasmania, forming the six states of Australia: New South Wales, Victoria, Western Australia, South Australia, Queensland, and Tasmania.
Canberra was officially named as the capital of Australia on 12 March 1913 after years of negotiation between New South Wales and Victoria.
